text.title: Quality Gates, Defect Triage, and Antipatterns
text.summary: Rules for deterministic quality gate execution, structured defect triage, and avoiding quality control antipatterns.

section.title: Quality Gate Rules

rule.statement: Quality gate decisions (release / hold / abort) require human approval. The gate script computes PASS/FAIL from thresholds; the agent emits a recommendation with evidence; a named human approves the release decision.
rule.rationale: Automated quality gate execution is appropriate for blocking merges; releasing to production requires human accountability that no automated system can substitute.

rule.statement: Every gate run must produce an immutable artifact with timestamp, inputs (CI run IDs, tool versions, threshold file version), and output (PASS/FAIL per criterion with evidence URLs). Audit evidence must be traceable, not curated post-hoc.
rule.rationale: Regulated programs (ISO, SOC 2, FDA) require evidence that the gate was run correctly at the time of release. Post-hoc evidence curation creates regulatory liability.

section.reference: Gate script: reads quality-plan.yaml thresholds and CI JSON, exits non-zero on failure

rule.statement: Every defect must be triaged against one severity rubric defined in quality-plan.yaml. Reject severity assignments made without reference to the rubric. Auto-close of duplicates requires human review — the agent recommends, the human merges.
rule.rationale: Agents inflate severity to be "safe" (everything S2/S3) or deflate to avoid escalation. A calibrated rubric with examples for each severity level is the only reliable anchor.

rule.statement: Defect reports must include steps-to-reproduce grounded on actual logs or traces. Agents must refuse to fabricate reproduction steps from description alone.
rule.rationale: Invented repro steps cannot be executed, blocking triage and fix verification. Grounding on actual artefacts (logs, traces, screenshots) is a non-negotiable quality gate on the defect report itself.

antipattern.description: Process metric becomes a gaming target (Goodhart's Law): teams hit the coverage percentage by testing trivial code paths while leaving critical paths untested.
antipattern.reason: Rotate which metrics gate vs. which inform; a metric used as a gate for more than two cycles is at risk of being gamed. Supplement coverage percentage with mutation testing or branch coverage.

antipattern.description: Agent writing "evidence" for a DoD item without a traceable artefact source.
antipattern.reason: Fabricated evidence creates audit liability. Every pass assertion must point to an immutable artefact URL (CI run, report file, scan SARIF). Refuse to pass any criterion without a concrete source.

antipattern.description: Defect logs sent to a third-party LLM without PII scrubbing.
antipattern.reason: Defect logs routinely contain user IDs, email addresses, payment data, and health information. Scrub before sending to any external model.
